originalTitle: US imposes sanctions on DR Congo ex-President Kabila alleging rebel support
title: Big News from DR Congo: America Asks for Peace!
content:
Hello, Amazing Adventurers!

Welcome back to your special news desk, all about our incredible continent, Africa! Today, we have a story from a very big and beautiful country called the Democratic Republic of Congo, or DR Congo for short. It's a land full of amazing animals, like gorillas and okapis, and wonderful, kind people.

Who is Joseph Kabila?

Once upon a time, not long ago, there was a man named Joseph Kabila. He used to be the 'head teacher' or 'captain' of the whole country, DR Congo. We call this person the President! He helped lead the country for many, many years. Now, there is a new President who is leading DR Congo.

America Makes a Special Request

Did you know there's a big country far away called America? It's also known as the United States. America is like a big friend to many countries around the world. Recently, America did something special regarding Joseph Kabila. They have put something called 'sanctions' on him. Now, that's a big word, but don't worry, we'll explain it simply!

What are 'Sanctions'?

Imagine you are playing with your friends, and someone isn't following the rules of the game. Maybe they keep trying to trip people or hide the ball. Your parents might say, 'Okay, you can't play with that special toy for a little while until you learn to play nicely.' Well, 'sanctions' are a bit like that for grown-ups and countries. It's when a country like America says, 'We think something isn't quite right, and we want to encourage people to follow the rules.' So, for Mr. Kabila, it means he might not be able to use his money in some special ways, or travel easily to certain places that America can control. It's a way for America to say, 'Please, let's make sure everyone helps to keep DR Congo peaceful and strong!'

Why Did America Do This?

America is saying that they think Joseph Kabila might be helping some people who are causing trouble in DR Congo. These people are sometimes called 'rebels.' Rebels are like groups of people who don't want to follow the country's rules and sometimes make things difficult or scary for others. America believes that by helping these groups, it might make it harder for the current government of DR Congo to do its job well and keep everyone safe and happy. They want to make sure everyone in DR Congo can live in peace and that the country's rules are followed. Did you know? DR Congo is the second-largest country in Africa! It's so big, you could fit many other countries inside it!

What Does 'Destabilise' Mean?

Another big word you might hear is 'destabilise.' Imagine you have a tall tower of blocks. If someone keeps shaking the table, the tower might fall over. That's like trying to 'destabilise' it. America is worried that if Mr. Kabila helps people who cause trouble, it could be like shaking the table for the country's government. They want the government to be strong and steady, like a perfectly built block tower, so it can take good care of all the people in DR Congo.

What Has Mr. Kabila Said?

When America shared its worries and put these 'sanctions' in place, they also waited to hear what Joseph Kabila would say. But so far, he hasn't said anything back about what America believes. It's like if your friend tells you they're worried about something you did, and you haven't answered them yet. Everyone is waiting to see what happens next. Did you know? DR Congo has amazing rainforests, just like a jungle, with lots of different plants and animals you can't find anywhere else in the world!

Why is Peace So Important?

When countries are peaceful, everyone can go to school, play with their friends, and grow up strong and healthy. When there is trouble, it can make it hard for people to live happily. That's why leaders and countries always try to make sure things are fair and peaceful. America wants to help make sure DR Congo stays peaceful, so children can learn and families can work together to make their country even better. They are trying to encourage everyone to work with the current leaders to keep the country strong and free from trouble.

Working Together for a Brighter Future

It's very important for everyone in a country – the leaders, the people, and even people who used to be leaders – to work together. When everyone follows the rules and wants what's best for the country, then wonderful things can happen! Did you know? DR Congo is very rich in special minerals like cobalt, which is used to make batteries for our phones and electric cars! It's a very important country for the whole world. This story reminds us that even big grown-ups and countries sometimes need to talk about rules and how to keep things fair and peaceful. We hope that everyone in DR Congo can continue to work towards a happy and bright future for all its wonderful children! Stay curious, stay kind, and remember to always follow the rules, just like we hope all the leaders will too! Did you know? The Congo River, which flows through DR Congo, is the deepest river in the world!
summary: America has put special rules, called sanctions, on Joseph Kabila, the former President of DR Congo, because they think he might be helping people who cause trouble, and they want him to help keep the country peaceful.
whyMatters: This is important because it's about keeping a big African country, DR Congo, safe and peaceful, so everyone can live happily and follow the rules, which is good for all the children and families there.

title: China scraps tariffs for all but one African nation

content: A shortage of fertiliser due to the Iran conflict could reduce crop yields and push prices higher, says the boss of Yara.

title: French PM fuels row with trip to buy baguettes
content: Sébastien Lecornu visited a boulangerie on Labour Day, defying unions who say 1 May must remain a mandatory rest day.

content: Manager Roberto de Zerbi urges Tottenham to "silence the voice inside of us" as the club battle to avoid relegation from the Premier League.

title: Saudi Arabian owners' desire for Newcastle 'unchanged'

title: Why was Mudryk given ban in secret and what happens now?
